rationale: The citing paper uses Kivetz, Urminsky, and Zheng (2006) as a core theoretical anchor for the goal-gradient hypothesis, including a direct definition quote and repeated use in the conceptual background. It positions its main contribution against that baseline by arguing that evaluation mode changes the effect of goal progress, and it interprets separate-evaluation findings as consistent with prior goal-gradient results. This is more than a passing citation list entry because the cited work helps frame hypotheses, expected direction of effects, and interpretation of when effects should or should not appear.
relation_description: The citing paper refines the goal-gradient account from Kivetz et al. (2006) by introducing evaluation mode as a boundary condition. It shows that while the near-goal preference can appear in separate evaluation, joint evaluation in charitable contexts can reverse preferences toward farther-from-goal targets.
